coredata.dc:title: Solid–liquid convertible hydrogel mediated by dual-functional cellulose nanofiber/Eu–dipicolinic acid/carbon dot complexes for high-precision sequential luminescence detection of cupric ion and oxytetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Yue, Yiying
Gu, Jiamin
Cheng, Wanli
Han, Guangping
Han, Jingquan
Jiang, Jianchun
coredata.dc:description: A portable, sensitive, rapid, specific, and environment-friendly polyvinyl alcohol–borax (PB) hydrogel fluorescence probe was synthesized for the sequential sensing of Cu2+ and oxytetracycline (OTC) in wastewater. The aminated 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinooxy-oxidized cellulose nanofibers/europium complexes/carbon dot (ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD) complex was added to the PB matrix. With the assistance of ATOCNs, the in-situ polymerized ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD complexes were well-dispersed in the hydrogel and enhanced the mechanical properties of the hydrogel. The prepared hydrogel could be converted from solid to liquid by adjusting the water content to reduce the deviations caused by the solid probe detection. The probe not only induced evident fluorescence quenching and instantaneous response to Cu2+ at 430 nm and OTC at 617 nm but also exhibited a high selectivity to Cu2+ and OTC. The detection limits for Cu2+ and OTC were 0.031 and 0.019 mg L−1, respectively. Fluorescence recover was achieved by adding S2− for CuS precipitate formation and for the thermal decomposition of OTC. After repeatedly recovering the fluorescence for 10 times, the fluorescence intensity at 430 nm was restored to 79% of the original intensity and that at 617 nm was stored to 95%. The results show that hydrogels have remarkable potential applicability in detecting Cu2+ and OTC in wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent probe
Hydrogel
Solid–liquid convertible
Cu2+ and oxytetracycline sensing
Fluorescence recovery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Rheological and reversibility of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel
Mechanism and results of Cu2+ and OTC detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Qing Lan Project of Jiangsu Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Qinglan Project of Jiangsu Province of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31901274 ), the 13th China Special Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2020T130303 ), the Postdoctoral Research Foundation of China ( 2019M661854 ), the Jiangsu Postdoctoral Research Foundation ( 2019K142 ), the 333 Project Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BRA2018337 ), and the Qing Lan Project of Jiangsu Province (2019).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of the PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel: (a) Formation mechanism of ATOCNs; (b) synthesis process and the 3D structural model of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD; (c) chemical bond of multi-complexation between PVA, ATOCN/(DPA)3/CD, and borax.

Rheological performance and compression performance of hydrogels at 25 °C: (a) angular frequency dependence of G′ and G″ for hydrogels; (b) angular frequency dependence of G* and η* for hydrogels; (c) angular frequency dependence of G′ and G″ for samples 1–5 with the introduction of different masses of water and photo of samples 1–5 (from left to right); (d) self-healing and reversible cross-links of prepared hydrogel.
Mechanism of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel in detecting Cu2+ and OTC, and the recovery of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD.

Fluorescence properties: (a) fluorescence emission spectra of ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD at different concentrations; (b) influence of excitation wavelength on the fluorescence emission spectra of ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD; (c) influence of pH on the fluorescence emission spectra of ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD at excitation wavelength of 275 nm; (d) fluorescence strength of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD induced by different Cu2+ concentrations; (e) linear graph of Cu2+ assayed by fluorescence quenching; (f) fluorescence intensity of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD in response to various metal ions; (g) fluorescence strength of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD induced by different OTC concentrations; (h) linear graph of OTC assayed by fluorescence quenching; (i) fluorescence intensity of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD in response to various antibiotics; (j) photo of red fluorescence quenching (top) and blue fluorescence quenching (bottom); (k) reproducibility and number of recovery times of the prepared hydrogel; (l) comparison of reproducibility and number of recovery times between the prepared hydrogel probe and other reported probes. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Solid–liquid convertible hydrogel mediated by dual-functional cellulose nanofiber/Eu–dipicolinic acid/carbon dot complexes for high-precision sequential luminescence detection of cupric ion and oxytetracycline

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yiying Yue and Jiamin Gu have contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A portable, sensitive, rapid, specific, and environment-friendly polyvinyl alcohol–borax (PB) hydrogel fluorescence probe was synthesized for the sequential sensing of Cu2+ and oxytetracycline (OTC) in wastewater. The aminated 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinooxy-oxidized cellulose nanofibers/europium complexes/carbon dot (ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD) complex was added to the PB matrix. With the assistance of ATOCNs, the in-situ polymerized ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD complexes were well-dispersed in the hydrogel and enhanced the mechanical properties of the hydrogel. The prepared hydrogel could be converted from solid to liquid by adjusting the water content to reduce the deviations caused by the solid probe detection. The probe not only induced evident fluorescence quenching and instantaneous response to Cu2+ at 430 nm and OTC at 617 nm but also exhibited a high selectivity to Cu2+ and OTC. The detection limits for Cu2+ and OTC were 0.031 and 0.019 mg L−1, respectively. Fluorescence recover was achieved by adding S2− for CuS precipitate formation and for the thermal decomposition of OTC. After repeatedly recovering the fluorescence for 10 times, the fluorescence intensity at 430 nm was restored to 79% of the original intensity and that at 617 nm was stored to 95%. The results show that hydrogels have remarkable potential applicability in detecting Cu2+ and OTC in wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution presents a major environmental problem because of its toxicity, persistence, and recalcitrance [1]. The excessive discharge of Cu2+ sewage into rivers, lakes, and oceans not only reduces water quality, posing an enormous threat to aquatic ecosystems, but is also a threat to human health, especially to hematological systems [1]. Biologically toxic antibiotics, such as oxytetracycline (OTC), inhibit normal microbial growth or reproduction in natural water. Long-term exposure to antibiotics may also bring about antibiotic-resistant superbugs [2]. Antibiotic-contaminated wastewater can directly enter the major sources of drinking water (surface water and groundwater), reducing its safety [2]. The accumulation of OTC in the human body can lead to allergic reactions, gastrointestinal dysfunction, and liver damage. Because trace Cu2+ and OTC in the environment can have serious impacts and are difficult to monitor, detecting metal ions and antibiotics in an accurate, and quick, and effective manner is crucial.

Over the last few years, various analytical methods for sensing metal ions and antibiotics have been reported. Advanced methods, including atomic absorption spectrometry, ion chromatography, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and surface plasmon resonance, have been used to detect metal ions. High-performance liquid chromatography, high-performance capillary electrophoresis, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, and mass spectrometry have also been used to test antibiotics. These methods possess good accuracy and sensitivity, but their application is hindered by the high cost of equipment, lack of well-trained technicians, and cumbersome sample pretreatment processes [2]. In contrast to these aforementioned techniques, fluorescent probe is a convenient and timely in-situ detection method that is low cost, enables visualization, and affords a rapid, sensitive, and selective response.

Rare-earth complexes are excellent fluorescent materials with the following favorable features: (1) long fluorescence lifetimes [3]; (2) narrow emission peaks (the half-peak width is approximately 10–15 nm), which is conducive to multicolor fluorescence imaging; and (3) high sensitivity because of their large Stokes shift that effectively reduces background interference [3]. Therefore, the development of a fluorescence detection method on the basis of lanthanide luminescence has become a widely researched topic. Trivalent Lanthanide ions (Ln3+) can readily form complexes with carboxylate ligands, which sensitize the intra-4f transitions of Ln3+ through energy transfer [3]. Among Ln3+, trivalent europium ions (Eu3+) form complexes with dipicolinic acid (Eu(DPA)3) which are red fluorescent materials with promising applications. However, the characteristic emissions of Eu3+ tend to be quenched by water molecules and is poorly dispersed in water, limiting their applications in aqueous environments [4]. An improved strategy is to prepare composites consisting of carbon dots (CDs) and Eu(DPA)3 [5]. CDs are a new type of spherical nanoscale carbon material used in various fields (e.g., biosensing, bioimaging, environmental monitoring, photocatalysis, photoelectric materials and energy) because of their excellent stability, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, environment-friendliness, and low cost [6]. CDs are easy to synthesize and can be prepared from multiple carbon sources, such as biomass, organic molecules, and other carbon-containing materials. The shells of CDs are rich in hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which are available for surface modification [7]. The low quantum yield and optical properties of CDs can be enhanced by atom doping, while a higher selectivity is obtained [7]. Although the theoretical explanation for the luminous mechanism is insufficient, the aforementioned advantages make CDs excellent candidate materials for fluorescent probes. To facilitate the portability and ease of in-situ measurement and recovery, the fluorescent probe should be loaded onto a solid carrier. However, solid carriers are generally opaque, and their thickness can cause fluorescence measure errors. In a previous report by our group [8], a polyvinyl alcohol-borax (PB) hydrogel was chosen as a substrate material for fluorescent probes. Reversible crosslinking between polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) chains and borax can lead to the solid-to-liquid phase conversion of PB. The PB hydrogel is nearly transparent, which meets the expectations of portability, accurate in-situ measurement and recovery. The key to improving the fluorescence intensity and cycle time is to uniformly disperse and fix the fluorescent materials in the hydrogel [8]. The strength of hydrogels is another problem that must be addressed in the development of solid fluorescent probes. To address these issues, the aminated 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinooxy(TEMPO)-oxidized cellulose nanofibers (ATOCNs) were synthesized by TEMPO-mediated oxidation of C6 groups [9] followed by the grafting of amino groups on the surface of TEMPO)-oxidized cellulose nanofiber (TOCN) chains. ATOCNs provide a natural framework for the attachment of Eu(DPA)3 and CDs through the amination reaction between the amino and carboxyl groups of ATOCNs, CDs and Eu(DPA)3 to form ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD complexes. These complexes are expected to not only have high fluorescence intensity but also significantly enhance the rheological properties and mechanical strength of the hydrogel.

In this study, a portable, highly accurate and reusable hydrogel sequential fluorescent probe is developed to monitor the concentrations of Cu2+ and OTC in water. An in-situ composite consisting of ATOCNs, CDs and Eu(DPA)3, which effectively enhances the mechanical properties of hydrogels and improves the accuracy of determination, was incorporated into the PB matrix. The obtained composite hydrogel is reversible and can be switched between solid and liquid states by adjusting the water content. This significantly reduces the errors arising from the detection of solid samples by a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The crosslinking mechanisms of the complexes and hydrogels were examined. The morphologies and microstructures of the ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD complex and PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel were investigated. In addition, the mechanisms of fluorescence detection, recovery, and selectivity were explored, and the reinforcement effects of the ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD complexes on the hydrogel were characterized via rheological and compressive tests. The selectivity and accuracy following multiple cycles of use were also determined. The proposed hydrogels have promising applications in the detection of Cu2+ and OTC in wastewater.

Borax (Na2B4O7·10H2O), PVA (146,000–186,000 g mol−1), sodium sulfide (Na2S), 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-piperidiniyl-1-oxyl (TEMPO), 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA), citric acid, N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), hydrochloric acid, urea, formaldehyde, copper chloride (CuCl2), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)·6H2O), and oxytetracycline (OTC) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co. Ltd.

The preparation method of ATOCNs, Eu(DPA)3, and CDs were described in Supporting information. The ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD complex was obtained by grafting the prepared ATOCNs, Eu(DPA)3, and CDs through typical polycondensation. In this method, 0.04 g Eu(DPA)3 was dispersed in a 10 mL NaAc/HAc (1 M) buffer. Then, 59.5 mg of EDC and 230 mg of NHS were dissolved in the resulting suspension by stirring for 2 h. Subsequently, a 20 mL ATOCN (1 wt %) suspension and 10 mL CD (0.2 wt%) solution were added to the mixture. After 12 h of reaction, dialysis tubes (membrane MWCO of 1000) were used to dialyze the ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD suspension against ultrapure water for 3 days. The obtained suspension was designated as ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD1. ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD1 was then diluted 4, 10, 50, and 100 times with distilled water and designated as ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD2-5, respectively.

PVA (1 g) was dissolved in 50 mL of ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD1 at 90 °C. Then, 0.2 g of borax was added to the mixture and stirred continuously until it gelatinized. The sample obtained was designated as PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD. The PB–ATOCN and PB hydrogels were synthesized via the same procedure, except that an ATOCN suspension and distilled water were used instead of ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD1.

The dynamic rheological performance of the prepared hydrogels was evaluated using a HAAKE Restress 600 rheometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA) with a 35 mm diameter parallel-plate geometry at 25 °C prior to the experiments. A strain of 1 rad s−1 was selected for the dynamic viscoelastic measurements. The shear storage modulus (G′) and loss modulus (G″) were tested at an angular frequency (ω) = 0.1–100 rad s−1. The complex modulus (G*) and complex viscosity (η*) were computed as follows:
(1)
G *
=
G
′ 2
+ G
″ 2
(2)
η * =
G *
ω

The luminescence responses of ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD and PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD to aqueous solutions of various metal cations and antibiotics were recorded by an LS-55 fluorescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The excitation wavelength, emission slit width, and the scanning speed were 275 nm, 5 nm, and 200 nm min−1, respectively. The effects of the sample concentration, wavelength, and pH on the fluorescence intensity were investigated. The standard curves of the Cu2+ and OTC content were determined by testing fluorescence intensity of the PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD in different concentrations of Cu2+ and OTC solutions. The limit of detection (LOD) was evaluated by Eq (3):
(3)
L O D = 3
S b
K
where Sb is the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements and K is the slope of standard curves.

The fabrication process and mechanism of the PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel are shown in Fig. 1 . The morphology and FTIR spectra of ATOCNs are shown in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2a, respectively. Firstly, TOCNs were synthesized by the oxidation of the sheer number of C6-hydroxyl groups to C6-carboxyl groups on the surface of cellulose microfibers in the TEMPO system, as reported in our previous work [8]. Then, the nanoscale ATOCNs suspension was prepared by introducing a functional group containing –NH2 groups to the TOCN chains (Fig. 1a). ATOCNs existed as a stable colloidal suspension without any aggregation owing to the electrostatic repulsion of anionic charges (–OH-, –COO-) on the surface. As shown in Fig. 1b, the CDs that emitted visible blue fluorescence were prepared with urea and citric acid by the hydrothermal method, while Eu(DPA)3 with red fluorescence was synthesized by Eu(NO3)·6H2O and DPA through refluxing. Composites consisting of CDs, Eu(DPA)3 and ATOCNs were prepared to overcome the tendency of Eu3+ emissions to be quenched by water molecules as well as their poor dispersion in water [4]. The synthesis mechanism of the ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD is shown in Fig. 1c. The carboxyl groups of Eu(DPA)3 and the carboxyl groups and amino groups of CDs and ATOCNs formed ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD complex through a dehydration condensation reaction in an NHS/EDC buffer. The morphology of CDs, Eu(DPA)3, and ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD complexes are present in Fig. S1. The average diameter of the CDs and Eu(DPA)3 is displayed in Table S1, the zeta potential and the dimension parameters of ATOCNs are shown in Table S2 and Table S3, respectively. Table S4 shows the size Gaussian distribution of ATOCNs, CDs and Eu(DPA)3.

The ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD was dispersed in a PB matrix to form a PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel probe. The three-dimensional (3D) network structure of the hydrogel is shown in Fig. 1b. The microstructure of the freeze-dried PB–AOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD was observed by SEM (Fig. S1). The synthesis mechanism of the PB hydrogel is shown in Fig. 1c. The crosslinking reaction between borax and PVA was divided into three steps. First, borax (Na2B4O7) was hydrolyzed to boric acid (H3BO3). Then, H3BO3 was further dissociated to tetrahedral B(OH)4 - in water [10], which consisted of four equilateral triangles with B at the center and an –OH group at the four corners [10]. Finally, the borate ions reacted with the hydroxyl groups on the PVA chains to yield a reversible crosslinked network [8]. Reversible bonds enabled the conversion of the PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel between the solid and liquid states and endowed it with satisfactory recyclability and self-healing capabilities. The FTIR spectra of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogels are shown in Fig. S2b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rheological and reversibility of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Oscillatory measurementswere performed on PB, PB–ATOCN, and PB– ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogels at 25 °C to elucidate the effects of ATOCNs and ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD complexes on elasticity and viscosity properties of hydrogels. The linear viscoelastic region (LVR) was determined by dynamic strain sweep (Fig. S3). Fig. 2 a shows the G′ and G″ as a function of the angular frequency (ω) for the composite hydrogels within the LVR. Evidently, the crosslinking of PB, PB–ATOCN, and PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD are frequency-dependent. The PB-based hydrogels exhibited liquid-like behavior when G' < G″ and quasi-solid behavior when G' > G''. The symbol of gelation has generally been considered as the points of intersection of G' = G″, indicating the entanglement of polymer chains and the formation of a typical elastic and reversible 3 D crosslinked hydrogel network [11]. G′ gradually approaches G″ with the expansion of ω until a crossover point of G′ and G″ curves appears. This indicates the formation of an elastic gel network between the entanglement and crosslinking of polymer chains and ATOCN. Fig. 2b shows plots of the complex modulus (G*) and complex viscosity (η*) as functions of ω for each hydrogel sample. Both the G* and η* were in the order of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD > PB–ATOCN > PB within the entire ω range. This confirmed that the incorporation of ATOCNs and ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD complexes reinforced the PB hydrogel and improved the stiffness and viscoelasticity. Compression and physical properties of hydrogels are shown in Table S5 and Fig. S4.

Fig. 2c shows the G′ and G″ of hydrogels evenly dispersed in distilled water. PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel was labeled as sample1. The PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogels and distilled water in mass ratios of 3:1, 1:1, 1:2 and 1:3 was recorded as sample2, sample3, sample4, sample5, respectively. All the G′ and G″ of hydrogels were tested at ω = 1.0 Hz, strain = 1.0%, and temperature = 25 °C. Samples 1–3 presented a solid-like behavior (G' > G″), indicating that diol complexes (O–B–O bonds) were formed by two diol units on the PVA chains and borate ions [10]. This shows that gelation was completed and an elastic hydrogel network was formed. The G′ and G″ values decreased with the increase in water content, and the hydrogels changed from a solid-like (G' > G″) to liquid-like state (G' < G″) with the continued increase in water content. This phenomenon is explained by the reversibility of the crosslinking reaction of the diol complex between PVA and borax. The O–B–O bonds that connected the borax and PVA chains broke down after the addition of distilled water. As a result, the hydrogel could be redissolved in water and converted from a solid into a liquid by adjusting the water content. Because of the relatively large deviation of the fluorescence intensity of the solid sample measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer, this solid-to-liquid conversion of the hydrogel fluorescent probe improved the accuracy of fluorescence detection. As shown in Fig. 2d, two blocks of freshly cut PB hydrogels with two different colors were brought into contact. The interface between the two blocks of PB hydrogels was fully integrated in 20 s in the absence of any external force, revealing the excellent self-healing performance of the reversible crosslinked network of hydrogels. One possible reason for self-healing performance is the reversible bonds formed by complexation between hydroxyl and borate ions, which could be readily broken and reformed inside the hydrogels. In particular, the borate ions complexated quickly with the –OH of adjacent polymer chains, hence, hydrogels could completely self-heal within a short period of time, significantly improving their portability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism and results of Cu2+ and OTC detection
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of the PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel for the detection and reuse of Cu2+ and OTC is shown in Fig. 3 . Under ultraviolet light, the solution emitted a mauve fluorescence, which was derived from the red and blue fluorescence emitted by Eu(DPA)3 and CDs, respectively The most widely accepted fluorescence mechanisms of CDs are caused by the surface state, edge state, and their synergistic effect [12]. During the preparation process, CDs exhibited surface defects due to the introduction of functional groups (–COOH, –OH, C–H, C–N, and –NH2), forming the surface and edge states between the π and π* energy levels [12]. These surface defects affected the band gap between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital and the highest occupied molecular orbital, and ultimately determined the luminescence color. The fluorescence intensity and color of CDs are related to their particle size and surface functional groups [6]. CDs with an average diameter of 1.41 nm emitted a visible blue fluorescence. As the ligand, DPA provided an oxygen atom from the carboxyl group and a nitrogen atom from the pyridine ring to coordinate with Eu3+ and successfully synthesize the red highly fluorescent complex Eu(DPA)3. The luminescence mechanism is that the ligand (DPA) strongly absorbed the energy of the excited light and transferred it to the central Eu3+ by an “Antenna” effect that enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the central ion [3]. The energy between the lowest excited triplet of DPA and the lowest excited state level of the central Eu3+ (5D0) were well-matched, ensuring the effective transfer of energy from DPA to Eu3+, and prohibiting the energy from being transferred back. Eu(DPA)3 exhibited two emission peaks due to the 5D0→7F1 transition (weak emission peak) and 5D0→7F2 transition (strong emission peak) [13]. Among them, 5D0→7F2 transition is a typical electric dipole transition and the main cause of the red emission of the solution [13].

The red fluorescence emitted by Eu3+ was quenched, and the fluorescence color changed from mauve to blue upon exposure to the Cu2+ solution. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of Eu(DPA)3 by Cu2+ is that the chelation between Cu2+ and uncoordinated carboxylic acid groups in Eu(DPA)3 caused the energy transfer from DPA to Cu2+ through the O–Cu–O bond, which weakened the energy transferred to Eu3+ and the red fluorescence quenching [3]. With the addition of OTC, the blue fluorescence of CDs was quenched, and the fluorescence characteristics vanished. The blue fluorescence quenching mechanism is the inner-filter effect. The competitive absorption of excited light by OTCs weakened the fluorescence intensity of the CDs, leading to a decline in the fluorescence intensity [14].

Recovery is an important parameter to determine the reusability of the probe. Owing to the crosslinking reaction between borax and PVA chains was reversible, the O–B–O bonds broke down after the addition of distilled water and stirring, which converted the hydrogel to a liquid state [8]. Then, S2− was added to liquid mixture precipitate with Cu2+ to form CuS and remove Cu2+ from the solution. Consequently, the red fluorescence of Eu(DPA)3 quenched by Cu2+ gradually recovered. After restoring the blue fluorescence of the CDs by heating to decompose the OTC, the mixture was re-crosslinked by evaporating excess water to form hydrogels, thereby realizing the recycling of the hydrogel fluorescent probe. Because the thickness of solid samples cannot be controlled as precisely as that of liquid samples, a fluorescent probe based on the proposed reversible crosslinked PVA-borax complex could significantly reduce the error in detecting solid samples by fluorescence spectrophotometry.

The fluorescence properties of the PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel probe and its response to Cu2+ and OTC are shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4a shows the fluorescence emission spectra of ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD at different concentrations. ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD5 was chosen for the subsequent experiments due to its high fluorescence intensity. Evidently, the TOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD probe obtained emission peaks at 430, 594, and 617 nm, which were because of the π–π* and n–π* transitions of CDs, and 5D0→7F1 and 5D0→7F2 transitions of Eu(DPA)3, respectively [13]. ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD4 with a high fluorescence intensity from both CDs and Eu(DPA)3 was added to the hydrogel for subsequent experiments. Fig. 4b shows the fluorescence emission spectrum of the PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD at an excitation wavelength of 250–280 nm. Because the fluorescence intensities of Eu(DPA)3 were lower than those of CDs, the excitation wavelength of 275 nm, which is favorable to Eu(DPA)3 luminescence, was selected for the follow-up tests. Fig. 4c shows the influence of pH on the fluorescence intensity of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD. At the excitation of 275 nm, the emission peak at 430 nm from the CDs and at 594 and 617 nm from Eu(DPA)3 reached the maximum fluorescence intensity at pH = 8 and pH = 10, respectively. Hence, the fluorescent probe was only applicable to sensing alkali and neutral metal wastewaters. The pH-dependent fluorescence behavior originated from the amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups of CDs doped with N elements [15]. The amino group carried positive charges in acids, while the carboxyl group carried negative charges in the alkaline environment. The photoluminescence was affected because the charges repelled each other [6]. In addition, because of the existence of –OH, –COOH, and –NH2, the CDs were prone to H-aggregation and formed large aggregates in acid solution, resulting in the quenching of CDs [16]. Whereas in an alkaline environment, the –OH, –COOH and –NH2 could be linked to the chromophore groups of CDs, and caused n-π conjugation that effectively increased the absorption and led to an increase in the fluorescence intensity. For Eu(DPA)3, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased in an acidic aqueous solution owing to protonation of carboxylic acid ions. Their complexation capability with Eu3+ was weakened, resulting in the dissociation of Eu(DPA)3. Compared with CDs, the fluorescence strength of Eu(DPA)3 was relatively poor, therefore, a pH of 10 was chosen for the subsequent experiments.

As shown in Fig. 4d, the fluorescence intensity of the PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD fluorescent probe at 594 nm and 617 nm decreased with the increase in Cu2+ concentration owing to the quenching of the red fluorescence emitted by Eu(DPA)3. The fluorescent color of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD changed from mauve (a red-blue combination) to blue (upper part of Fig. 4j), which shows that the hydrogel fluorescent probe can be employed to detect Cu2+ in water. The standard curve for the determination of Cu2+ by fluorescence quenching of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel is shown in Fig. 4e. The relative fluorescence intensity log[(F0 – F)/F0] exhibited a linear relationship in the range of 0–6 mg L−1 Cu2+. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD and PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD–Cu2+, respectively. The linear regression equation is y = 0.13568x – 0.99639, and the linear correlation coefficient is 0.9964. The sensing limit of Cu2+ is 0.031 mg L−1, which is considerably lower than the acceptable concentration of Cu2+ in drinking water recommended by the World Health Organization (2 mg L−1) [17]. Compared with most reported sensors, the obtained fluorescence probe has a lower detection limit for Cu2+ (Table S6). Selectivity is an important criterion for appraising the availability and practicality of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD. The fluorescence intensity of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD upon exposure to 6 mg L−1 of various metal ions (Fe3+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Co2 +, Zr4+, Ag+, and Bi3+) and its response to Cu2+ under the interference of the above various metal ions were tested at 275 nm (Fig. 4f). However, the interference of Fe2+ is slight in practical applications because Fe2+ is unstable and easily oxidized to Fe3+ by oxygen in air. Fe3+ tend to form precipitates in alkaline test environments due to the incredibly small solubility product constant (2.79 × 10−39), which is much smaller than that of Cu2+ (2.2 × 10−20). The effects of Mn2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Co2 +, Pb2+, Zr4+, Ag+, and Bi3+ on the fluorescence intensity of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD could be negligible under the same conditions. These results show that PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogels possess a high response specificity to Cu2+ even when coexisting with other metal ions. Therefore, PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogels have potential applications in detecting Cu2+ in alkaline environments.

The fluorescence emission spectra of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD with different concentrations of OTC solutions at 275 nm are shown in Fig. 4g. The fluorescence intensity decreased as the OTC concentration increased. A photo of CDs emitting blue fluorescence and quenching by OTC is shown at the bottom of Fig. 4j. The standard OTC curve was obtained by detecting fluorescence intensity of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD at various OTC concentrations. The relative fluorescence intensity log[(F0 – F)/F0] exhibited a linear relationship with OTC in the concentration range of 0.1–0.5 mg L−1. The linear regression equation is y = 2.41398x – 1.6932, and the linear correlation coefficient is 0.9986 (Fig. 4h). The OTC detection limit is 0.019 mg L−1. It can be seen from Table S7 that PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD has a lower detection limit for OTC than most of the reported sensors. Fig. 4i shows the fluorescence intensity of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD used to detect 0.9 mg L−1 of various antibiotics (tetracycline (TC), curcumin (CUR), erythromycin (EM), trimethoprim (TMP), chlortetracycline (CTC), ciprofloxacin (CIP) and streptomycin sulfate (SS) and the response of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD to OTC under the interference of these antibiotics. As shown in Fig. 4i, TC, CUR, EM, TMP, CTC, CIP and SS had a negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD, demonstrating that the PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel possessed a high response specificity to OTC. Therefore, PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD has potential applicability in sensing OTC in aqueous environments.

The reproducibility and number of cycles of the PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel are shown in Fig. 4k. The reversal of the fluorescence intensity of the probe could be repeated more than 10 times. After 10 repetitions, the fluorescence intensities of Eu(DPA)3 at 617 nm and that of CDs at 430 nm were maintained at 95% and 79%, respectively. Fig. 4l shows a comparison of the reproducibility and cycle times of the synthesized hydrogel probe in this work with those of other reported probes [18–27]. PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD outperformed most reported sensors in terms of reproducibility and cycle time.

In this study, a portable, sensitive, self-healing and reusable PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel probe was fabricated for the sequential sensing of Cu2+ and OTC in aqueous environments. The synthesis of ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD complexes not only promoted the dispersion of Eu(DPA)3 in the hydrogel matrix, but also enhanced the strength of the hydrogel matrix (the stress value of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD was approximately 2.33 times that of PB hydrogel). The linear regression equation of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD to Cu2+ at 617 nm was y = 0.13568x – 0.99639, and sensing limit was 0.031 mg L−1. The linear regression equation of the OTC at 430 nm was y = 2.41398x −1.6932 and the detection limit was 0.019 mg L−1. After adding distilled water, the O–B–O bonds broke down and the solid-to-liquid conversion of the hydrogel was achieved, which improved the accuracy of fluorescence detection. The resulting PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD hydrogel has a high specificity to Cu2+ and OTC. The repeated detection by the hydrogel probe was carried out by adding S2− for the formation of CuS precipitates and thermal decomposition of OTC. After repeatedly used for 10 times, the fluorescence intensity of PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD for detecting Cu2+ and OTC was recovered to 79% and 95% of the original intensity, respectively. Hence, the prepared PB–ATOCN/Eu(DPA)3/CD has remarkable application potential in sensing Cu2+ and OTC in wastewater.

Conceptualization, Yiying Yue and Jiamin Gu; Formal analysis, Yiying Yue, Jiamin Gu, Wanli Cheng and Guangping Han; Funding acquisition, Yiying Yue, Jingquan Han and Jianchun Jiang; Investigation, Jiamin Gu, Wanli Cheng and Guangping Han; Supervision, Yiying Yue, Jingquan Han and Jianchun Jiang; Validation, Wanli Cheng and Guangping Han; Writing – original draft, Yiying Yue and Jiamin Gu.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
